worth_it_explanation: A publisher's Amazon Publisher Services account and seat structure decides how reporting and payments roll up, and how cleanly the publisher's identity appears to buyers through sellers.json. Fragmented or duplicate seats split the picture across entities that should be one, and orphaned integrations keep a seat live for inventory the publisher no longer runs. The check is a review of structure the publisher already has rather than a rebuild, so it costs reading time, and the finding points to a concrete consolidation or closure the publisher can act on.
intro: Amazon Publisher Services (APS) routes a publisher's inventory through a seller account that is declared in Amazon's sellers.json file, so the account and seat structure decides how cleanly the publisher appears in the supply chain and how reporting and payments roll up. This subcategory is about the structure itself, whether the seats and entities are organized cleanly and whether any seat or integration has been left running with no inventory behind it. These checks confirm the account maps to the publisher's real structure rather than a tangle of fragments accumulated over time.

audit_checks.title: Seats or entities are fragmented or duplicated across the account
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When one publisher's inventory is split across several Amazon Publisher Services seats or entity records that should be a single account, reporting and payments fragment along those lines and the publisher's seller_id picture in sellers.json reflects the split rather than the real business. Buyers reading the supply chain see a less coherent seller, and the publisher loses a consolidated view of revenue, so the fragmentation works against both transparency and internal reporting.

educate.base:
Amazon Publisher Services (APS) gives a publisher a seller account, and Amazon declares that account in its sellers.json file so buyers can trace who is selling the inventory. The account carries the publisher's seats and entity records, and the way those are organized decides how reporting rolls up, how payments are grouped, and how coherent the publisher looks to a buyer reading the supply chain. A clean structure maps one publisher to one account with the seats it actually needs. A fragmented structure spreads a single publisher across several seats or duplicate entity records that should have been one.

Fragmentation tends to accumulate rather than arrive all at once. A site added through a separate signup, an entity record created for a one-off integration, or a seat set up during a migration and never folded back in each leaves a fragment behind. Once the inventory is split, reporting splits with it, so the publisher reads revenue across several places rather than one, and payments group along the same lines. The seller picture in sellers.json follows the structure, so a buyer cross-referencing the publisher's ads.txt against Amazon's sellers.json may see more seller records than the business actually warrants.

The transparency standards make the stakes concrete. The IAB Tech Lab sellers.json specification holds that a seller_id should represent a single entity, and buyers reading ads.txt, sellers.json, and the OpenRTB SupplyChain object together expect the seller picture to map to a real business. A fragmented account works against that expectation even when nothing is misconfigured because the structure no longer reflects the publisher cleanly.

A few signs point to fragmentation or duplication:
  • The publisher's inventory is split across more than one Amazon Publisher Services seat or entity record when a single account would cover it
  • Reporting and payments arrive grouped in ways that do not match how the publisher thinks about its own sites or business units
  • Amazon's sellers.json shows more than one seller record tied to the publisher's domains, suggesting the same business is declared more than once
You may want to confirm the account structure maps to the publisher's real organization. One approach is to list every seat and entity record, match each to the inventory it actually carries, and consolidate the ones that represent the same business.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media business that runs several titles or sections, it can be tempting to set up a seat per title, but that splits reporting and the seller picture across records that one account could carry. You may want to confirm whether the titles are genuinely separate legal entities or one business, and consolidate the seats where a single entity owns them.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business, a seat may have been created separately for an editorial property and a shopping property even though one company owns both. You may want to check whether the two represent the same legal entity because a single account keeps reporting and the sellers.json picture aligned with the business rather than split by property.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed configuration documentation for seat and account setup is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every Amazon Publisher Services seat and entity record, match each to the inventory it carries, and cross-reference Amazon's sellers.json against the publisher's ads.txt to see how many seller records tie back to the business.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services console and list every seat and entity record under the publisher's account.
For each seat, record the inventory or sites it carries and the legal entity it represents.
Note any case where two seats or entity records carry inventory for the same business, which points to a fragment that could consolidate.

sellers_json.label: Amazon sellers.json and publisher ads.txt
steps: Open Amazon's sellers.json file and search for the seller records tied to the publisher's domains and account.
Open the publisher's ads.txt file and list the Amazon seller account IDs it authorizes.
Compare the two and record whether the seller records map to one coherent business or whether the same publisher appears across several records.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every seat and entity record alongside the inventory and legal entity behind each, plus the seller records found in Amazon's sellers.json. A structure where each seat maps to a distinct entity and the sellers.json picture reflects the real business passes. A structure where one publisher's inventory is split across seats or duplicate entity records that should be a single account is a fail recorded with the seats and records that represent the same business.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each seat, the inventory and entity behind it, and the matching sellers.json records above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has its Amazon Publisher Services (APS) inventory split across more than one seat or entity record when a single account would cover the business. Fragmentation splits reporting and payments along those lines and shows more seller records in Amazon's sellers.json than the business warrants, which works against the transparency buyers expect when they read ads.txt, sellers.json, and the supply chain together. {{voice_recommend}} listing every seat and entity record, matching each to the inventory and legal entity behind it, and consolidating the records that represent the same business into one account. After the consolidation, recheck the sellers.json picture to confirm the seller records map cleanly to the publisher.

audit_checks.title: Inactive or orphaned seats and integrations are left open
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A seat or integration left active for inventory the publisher no longer runs keeps a seller record live in Amazon's sellers.json and an authorization line live in ads.txt for supply that no longer exists. That widens the surface a bad actor could spoof and leaves the publisher's declared supply chain out of step with reality, so closing the orphaned records is a housekeeping step that tightens transparency.

educate.base:
An Amazon Publisher Services (APS) seat or integration represents a live path between a publisher's inventory and the buyers in Amazon's marketplace. When the publisher retires a site, sunsets a property, or moves inventory off the integration, the seat behind it does not close on its own. It keeps a seller record live in Amazon's sellers.json and, through the publisher's ads.txt, keeps an authorization line live for supply that no longer exists. An orphaned seat is a path the publisher has stopped using but has not shut.

The cost is mostly about a supply chain that no longer matches reality. The transparency standards exist so a buyer can trace a bid request to a real seller of real inventory. A live seller record for inventory that has been retired widens the gap between what the publisher declares and what the publisher actually sells. It also leaves a record a bad actor could try to spoof because an authorization line that points at dormant supply is harder to reconcile than one that maps to live inventory.

Keeping the structure current is a matter of closing what is no longer in use. When inventory leaves an integration, the seat behind it and the matching ads.txt line should be reviewed and removed if nothing else routes through them. The audit question is whether every live seat and authorization line still carries inventory, or whether some are holding open paths the publisher abandoned.

A few signs point to orphaned seats or integrations:
  • A seat is active for a site or property the publisher has retired or moved off Amazon Publisher Services
  • The publisher's ads.txt still authorizes an Amazon seller account that no longer carries live inventory
  • No one can say what inventory a given seat or integration currently serves, which suggests it has been abandoned rather than maintained
You may want to confirm every live seat and authorization line still maps to inventory the publisher runs today. One approach is to match each seat and each ads.txt authorization against current inventory and close the ones that no longer carry any.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media business that has launched and retired sections or microsites over time, a seat set up for a now-closed property can stay live long after the inventory is gone. You may want to walk the list of seats against the sites the business actually runs today and close the ones tied to retired properties.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content business that has consolidated or sunset storefronts, an integration created for a property that no longer exists can keep a seller record live. You may want to confirm each seat maps to a property the business still operates and remove the records left behind by closed storefronts.

educate.learn_more_note: Amazon Publisher Services's detailed documentation for closing or deactivating a seat is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review every active Amazon Publisher Services seat and every Amazon authorization line in the publisher's ads.txt, then match each against the inventory the publisher runs today to find any that no longer carry live supply.

aps_console.label: Amazon Publisher Services console
steps: Open the Amazon Publisher Services console and list every active seat and integration.
For each one, record the inventory or site it currently serves, and flag any where no current inventory can be named.
Note any seat tied to a property the publisher has retired or moved off Amazon Publisher Services.

ads_txt.label: Publisher ads.txt
steps: Open the publisher's ads.txt file and list every authorization line that points at an Amazon seller account.
Match each line against a live seat carrying current inventory, and flag any that point at retired or moved inventory.
Record the authorization lines that no longer map to live supply as candidates for removal.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records every active seat and every Amazon authorization line in ads.txt alongside the inventory each currently serves. A structure where every live seat and authorization line maps to inventory the publisher runs today passes. A structure that keeps seats active or ads.txt lines authorized for retired or moved inventory is a fail recorded with the orphaned seats and authorization lines found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active seat, its current inventory, and the matching ads.txt authorization lines above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} keeps Amazon Publisher Services (APS) seats or ads.txt authorization lines active for inventory it no longer runs. An orphaned seat keeps a seller record live in Amazon's sellers.json and an authorization line live in ads.txt for supply that no longer exists, which widens the gap between the declared supply chain and reality and leaves a record a bad actor could try to spoof. {{voice_recommend}} matching every active seat and every Amazon authorization line against the inventory the publisher serves today, then closing the seats and removing the ads.txt lines that no longer carry live supply. After the cleanup, recheck sellers.json and ads.txt to confirm the live records map to current inventory.
